subject: Physics
q: A fighter jet is flying horizontally at a certain altitude with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. When it passes directly overhead an anti-aircraft gun, a bullet is fired from the gun, at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> with the horizontal, to hit the jet. If the bullet speed is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>°</mo></math>.
question: A fighter jet is flying horizontally at a certain altitude with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. When it passes directly overhead an anti-aircraft gun, a bullet is fired from the gun, at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> with the horizontal, to hit the jet. If the bullet speed is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>°</mo></math>.
solution:
In order to hit, horizontal velocity of both moving object should be same,

V1=V2cosθ ⇒200=400cosθ

⇒cosθ=12 ⇒θ=60°

subject: Physics
q: Two balls with same mass and initial velocity, are projected at different angles in such a way that maximum height reached by first ball is 8 times higher than that of the second ball. $T_1$ and $T_2$ are the total flying times of first and second ball, respectively, then the ratio of $T_1$ and $T_2$ is :
question: Two balls with same mass and initial velocity, are projected at different angles in such a way that maximum height reached by first ball is 8 times higher than that of the second ball. $T_1$ and $T_2$ are the total flying times of first and second ball, respectively, then the ratio of $T_1$ and $T_2$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, }\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_1=8 \times\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta_1}{2 \mathrm{~g}}=8 \times \frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta_2}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta_1=2 \sqrt{2} \sin \theta_2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta_1 / \mathrm{g}}{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta_2 / \mathrm{g}}=\frac{\sin \theta_1}{\sin \theta_2}=2 \sqrt{2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A clock has a continuously moving second's hand of 0.1m length. The average acceleration of the tip of the hand (in units of ms-2 ]) is of the order of :
question: A clock has a continuously moving second's hand of 0.1m length. The average acceleration of the tip of the hand (in units of ms-2 ]) is of the order of :
solution:
The instantaneous acceleration of tip is given by

ac=ω2R=2π602×0.1=1900≈10-3

subject: Physics
q: The maximum vertical height to which a man can throw a ball is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>136</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum horizontal distance upto which he can throw the same ball is
question: The maximum vertical height to which a man can throw a ball is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>136</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum horizontal distance upto which he can throw the same ball is
solution:
Maximum height is achieved when the projection is along the vertical direction,

Hmax=v22 g=136 m

Now, for maximum range, angle of projection should be 45°. Therefore,

Rmax=v2 g=2Hmax

=2136

=272 m

subject: Physics
q: A ball of mass 100 g is projected with velocity $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at $60^{\circ}$ with horizontal. The decrease in kinetic energy of the ball during the motion from point of projection to highest point is
question: A ball of mass 100 g is projected with velocity $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at $60^{\circ}$ with horizontal. The decrease in kinetic energy of the ball during the motion from point of projection to highest point is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2 \\ & \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\mathrm{v} \cos 60^{\circ}\right)^2=\frac{1}{8} m v^2 \\ & \Delta \mathrm{k}=\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{3}{8} \mathrm{mv}^2=\frac{3}{8} \times 0.1 \times 400=15 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The trajectory of a projectile near the surface of the earth is given as <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If it were launched at an angle <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> with speed <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> then <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo></math>
question: The trajectory of a projectile near the surface of the earth is given as <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If it were launched at an angle <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> with speed <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> then <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo></math>
solution:
y=2x-9x2
Comparing above equation with general equation of trajectory of projectile y=xtan⁡θ1-xR
tan θ=2
⇒sin θ=25 or cos θ=15
θ=sin-125 or θ=cos-115
And R = 2/9
R=v02sin⁡2θg=29
After substituting the value for g and sin 2θ. We get v0=35 m/s

subject: Physics
q: A modern grand-prix racing car of mass m is travelling on a flat track in a circular arc of radius R with a speed v. If the coefficient of static friction between the tyres and the track is μs, then the magnitude of negative lift FL acting downwards on the car is:
question: A modern grand-prix racing car of mass m is travelling on a flat track in a circular arc of radius R with a speed v. If the coefficient of static friction between the tyres and the track is μs, then the magnitude of negative lift FL acting downwards on the car is:
solution:
μsN=mv2R

N=mv2μsR=mg+FL

FL=mv2μsR-mg

subject: Physics
q: A particle starts from the origin at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> with an initial velocity of <math><mn>3.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and moves in the <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></math> plane with a constant acceleration <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>6.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math> coordinate of the particle at the instant when its <math><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math> coordinate is <math><mn>32</mn><mi></mi><mi>m</mi></math> is <math><mi>D</mi></math> meters. The value of <math><mi>D</mi></math> is:
question: A particle starts from the origin at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> with an initial velocity of <math><mn>3.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and moves in the <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></math> plane with a constant acceleration <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>6.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math> coordinate of the particle at the instant when its <math><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math> coordinate is <math><mn>32</mn><mi></mi><mi>m</mi></math> is <math><mi>D</mi></math> meters. The value of <math><mi>D</mi></math> is:
solution:
Sy=uyt+12ayt2
32=0+12×4t2⇒t=4 s
Sx=uxt+12axt2
=3×4+12×6×16
=60 m

subject: Physics
q: A projectile can have the same range $R$ for two angles of projection. If $T_1$ and $T_2$ be the time of flights in the two cases, then the product of the two time of flights is directly proportional to
question: A projectile can have the same range $R$ for two angles of projection. If $T_1$ and $T_2$ be the time of flights in the two cases, then the product of the two time of flights is directly proportional to
solution:
Range is same for complimentary angles. $\mathrm{T}_1=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$ and $\mathrm{T}_2=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin (90-\theta)}{\mathrm{g}}$ and $R=\frac{u^2 \sin 2 \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$
$$
\therefore \mathrm{T}_1 \mathrm{~T}_2=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{g}} \times \frac{2 \mathrm{u} \cos \theta}{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{g}} \text {. }
$$

subject: Physics
q: A vehicle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving along a levelled curved road of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> with angular velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The centripetal force acting on the vehicle is:
question: A vehicle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving along a levelled curved road of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> with angular velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The centripetal force acting on the vehicle is:
solution:
The centripetal force is given by 

F=Mv2R=MRω2

The given data is 

M=200 kgR=70 mω=0.2 rad s-1

The magnitude of the force is 

F=200×70×0.22 N

⇒F=560 N

subject: Physics
q:
A particle is projected at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ from horizontal at a speed of $60 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The height traversed by the particle in the first second is $\mathrm{h}_0$ and height traversed in the last second, before it reaches the maximum height, is $h_1$. The ratio $h_0: h_1$ is _________
[Take, $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ]
question:
A particle is projected at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ from horizontal at a speed of $60 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The height traversed by the particle in the first second is $\mathrm{h}_0$ and height traversed in the last second, before it reaches the maximum height, is $h_1$. The ratio $h_0: h_1$ is _________
[Take, $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ]
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{S}_1=30 \times 1-\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 1=25 \\ & \mathrm{~S}_3=30+\left(\frac{-10}{2}\right) \times(2 \times 3-1)=5 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~S}_1}{\mathrm{~S}_3}=\frac{25}{5}=5\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Projectiles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are thrown at angles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with vertical respectively from top of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> high tower. If their times of flight are same, the ratio of their speeds of projection <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is:
question: Projectiles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are thrown at angles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with vertical respectively from top of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> high tower. If their times of flight are same, the ratio of their speeds of projection <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is:
solution:
For same time of flight, vertical component of velocity of projection must be same. Therefore,

vAsin45°=vBsin30°⇒vAvB=1212⇒vAvB=12

subject: Physics
q: A man in a car at location Q on a straight highway is moving with speed $\mathrm{v}$. He decides to reach a point $P$ in a field at a distance $d$ from highway (point $M$ ) as shown in the figure.Speed of the car in the field is half to that on the highway. What should be the distance RM, so that the time taken to reach $P$ is minimum?
question: A man in a car at location Q on a straight highway is moving with speed $\mathrm{v}$. He decides to reach a point $P$ in a field at a distance $d$ from highway (point $M$ ) as shown in the figure.Speed of the car in the field is half to that on the highway. What should be the distance RM, so that the time taken to reach $P$ is minimum?
solution:
Let the car turn of the highway at a distance ' $x$ ' from the point $\mathrm{M}$. So, $\mathrm{RM}=x$ And if speed of car in field is $v$, then time taken by the car to cover the distance $Q R=$ $Q M-x$ on the highway, $$ t_1=\frac{Q M-x}{2 v} $$ Time taken to travel the distance ' $R P^{\prime}$ in the field $$ t_2=\frac{\sqrt{d^2+x^2}}{v} $$ Total time elapsed to move the car from $Q$ to $P$ $$ t=t_1+t_2=\frac{Q M-x}{2 v}+\frac{\sqrt{d^2+x^2}}{v} $$
$$ \text { or } x=\frac{d}{\sqrt{2^2-1}}=\frac{d}{\sqrt{3}} $$

subject: Physics
q: A shell is fired from a fixed artillery gun with an initial speed u such that it hits the target on the ground at a distance <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> from it. If <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> are the values of the time taken by it to hit the target in two possible ways, the product <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is:
question: A shell is fired from a fixed artillery gun with an initial speed u such that it hits the target on the ground at a distance <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> from it. If <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> are the values of the time taken by it to hit the target in two possible ways, the product <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is:
solution:
We know for complementary angle range is same for same projection speed.
t1=2usin(θ)g ….. (i)
t2=2usin(90-θ)g ….. (ii)
R=2u2sinθcosθg .… (iii)
From equation (i) and (ii)
t1t2=4 U2sinθ cosθg2
(Using the value of R from equation iii)
t1t2=2Rg

subject: Physics
q: A helicopter is flying horizontally with a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> at an altitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> has to drop a food packet for a man on the ground. What is the distance of helicopter from the man when the food packet is dropped ?
question: A helicopter is flying horizontally with a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> at an altitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> has to drop a food packet for a man on the ground. What is the distance of helicopter from the man when the food packet is dropped ?
solution:
The aeroplane imparts v velocity to the food packet horizontally when dropping. Horizontal range of food packet =v×2hg
Also, Horizontal distance, travelled by aeroplane =v×2hg thus, vertical distance between aeroplane and person =h
distance =2v2 h g+h2

subject: Physics
q: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is thrown vertically upward. Another ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is thrown an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> with the vertical. Both the balls stay in air for the same period of time. The ratio of the heights attained by the two balls respectively is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is thrown vertically upward. Another ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is thrown an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> with the vertical. Both the balls stay in air for the same period of time. The ratio of the heights attained by the two balls respectively is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
When the first ball of mass m is projected vertically upwards, the second ball of mass 2m makes an angle θ with the vertical.

The time of flight is the same for both the balls.

Then, T1=T2 ⇒2u1g=2u2sinθg

⇒u1=u2sinθ    ...1

Maximum height of projectile is given by H=u2sin2θ2g

Ratio of the heights is H1H2=u12u22sin2θ    ...2

From equation 1 and 2, we have 

⇒H1H2=1.

Thus, x=1.

subject: Physics
q:
In a spring gun having spring constant 100 N m-1 a small ball B of mass 100 g is put in its barrel (as shown in figure) by compressing the spring through 0.05 m. There should be a box placed at a distance d on the ground so that the ball falls in it. If the ball leaves the gun horizontally at a height of 2 m above the ground. The value of d is m.
g=10 m s-2
question:
In a spring gun having spring constant 100 N m-1 a small ball B of mass 100 g is put in its barrel (as shown in figure) by compressing the spring through 0.05 m. There should be a box placed at a distance d on the ground so that the ball falls in it. If the ball leaves the gun horizontally at a height of 2 m above the ground. The value of d is m.
g=10 m s-2
solution:
12kx2=12mv2
Kx2=mv2
v=xkm=0.051000.1=0.05×1010
v=0.510
From h=12gt2
t=2 h g=2×210=210
∴ d=vt=0.510×210=1 m

subject: Physics
q: A stone is projected at angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the horizontal. The ratio of kinetic energy of the stone at point of projection to its kinetic energy at the highest point of flight will be :
question: A stone is projected at angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the horizontal. The ratio of kinetic energy of the stone at point of projection to its kinetic energy at the highest point of flight will be :
solution:
Let stone is projected with initial velocity v.

Kinetic energy of the stone at point of projection is KE1=12mv2.

Now, velocity of stone at maximum height is =vcos30°, so kinetic energy at that point is KE2=12mvcos30°2

Hence, the ratio KE1KE2=12mv212mvcos30°2=43

question: A particle is projected at $60^{\circ}$ to the horizontal with a kinetic energy $\mathrm{K}$. The kinetic energy at the highest point is
solution: No Solution Available

subject: Physics
q: Two projectiles are fired with same initial speed from same point on ground at angles of \(\left(45^{\circ}-\alpha\right)\) and \(\left(45^{\circ}+\alpha\right)\), respectively, with the horizontal direction. The ratio of their maximum heights attained is :
question: Two projectiles are fired with same initial speed from same point on ground at angles of \(\left(45^{\circ}-\alpha\right)\) and \(\left(45^{\circ}+\alpha\right)\), respectively, with the horizontal direction. The ratio of their maximum heights attained is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{Max}}=\frac{(\mathrm{u} \sin \theta)^2}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\ & \frac{\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_1}{\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_2}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2(45-\alpha)}{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2(45+\alpha)} \\ & =\frac{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \alpha-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin \alpha\right)^2}{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \alpha+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin \alpha\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{1-\sin 2 \alpha}{1+\sin 2 \alpha}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A body of mass 10 kg is projected at an angle of 45° with the horizontal. The trajectory of the body is observed to pass through a point 20,10. If T is the time of flight, then its momentum vector, at time t=T2, is _____ .
[Take g=10 m s-2]
question:
A body of mass 10 kg is projected at an angle of 45° with the horizontal. The trajectory of the body is observed to pass through a point 20,10. If T is the time of flight, then its momentum vector, at time t=T2, is _____ .
[Take g=10 m s-2]
solution:
It is given that the projectile passes through 20,10 and angle of projection is θ=45°. Using the trajectory equation,

y=xtanθ-gx22u2cos2θ

⇒10=20-10100u2

⇒u=20 m s-1

Now the time of flight of projectile,

T=2usinθg

⇒T=220210=22 s⇒T2=2 s

Now velocity of the projectile at any time t can be written as,

v→=ucosθi^+usinθ-gtj^

⇒v→=102i^+(102-102]j^ m s-1

Therefore, momentum p→=Mv→=1002i^+1002-200j^ N s

subject: Physics
q: A ball is projected with kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>, at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the horizontal. The kinetic energy of this ball at the highest point of its flight will become :
question: A ball is projected with kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>, at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the horizontal. The kinetic energy of this ball at the highest point of its flight will become :
solution:
E=12mu2

At the Highest point, velocity is equal to the horizontal component of the velocity of projection. Therefore,

v=ucos60°=u2

∴ Kinetic energy at the topmost point =12 mu22=E4

subject: Physics
q: A river is flowing from west to east direction with speed of $9 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$. If a boat capable of moving at a maximum speed of $27 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$ in still water, crosses the river in half a minute, while moving with maximum speed at an angle of $150^{\circ}$ to direction of river flow, then the width of the river is :
question: A river is flowing from west to east direction with speed of $9 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$. If a boat capable of moving at a maximum speed of $27 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$ in still water, crosses the river in half a minute, while moving with maximum speed at an angle of $150^{\circ}$ to direction of river flow, then the width of the river is :
solution:
$\therefore \mathrm{V}_{\perp}=$ river flow $=27 \times \cos 60^{\circ}=\frac{27}{2} \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$.
Time taken $=30 \mathrm{sec}$.
$\therefore \mathrm{S}=\mathrm{Vt}=\frac{27}{2} \times \frac{5}{18} \times 30 \mathrm{~m}=112.5 \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Physics
q: Two projectiles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are thrown with initial velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal respectively. The ratio of their ranges respectively is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math>
question: Two projectiles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are thrown with initial velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal respectively. The ratio of their ranges respectively is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
The range R1 of the first projectile is given by

R1=u12sin2θ1g   ...(1)

The range R2 of the second projectile is given by

R2=u22sin2θ2g   ...(2)

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) and simplify to obtain the ratio of the ranges.

R1R2=u12sin2θ1gu22sin2θ2g= u12sin2θ1u22sin2θ2   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required ratio.

 R1R2=402×sin60°602×sin120°=49

subject: Physics
q: An object is projected in the air with initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math>. The projectile motion is such that the horizontal range <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, is maximum. Another object is projected in the air with a horizontal range half of the range of first object. The initial velocity remains same in both the case. The value of the angle of projection, at which the second object is projected, will be _____  degree.
question: An object is projected in the air with initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math>. The projectile motion is such that the horizontal range <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, is maximum. Another object is projected in the air with a horizontal range half of the range of first object. The initial velocity remains same in both the case. The value of the angle of projection, at which the second object is projected, will be _____  degree.
solution:
The maximum range is given by, Rmax=u2sin245°g=u2 g.

For the range to be half of the maximum range,

Rmax2=u22 g=u2sin2θg

⇒sin2θ=12

⇒2θ=30°or 2θ=150°

⇒θ=15°,75°

Therefore, answer is 15 or 75

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass $M$ thrown horizontally with velocity $v$ from the top of the tower of height $H$ touches the ground at a distance of $100 \mathrm{~m}$ from the foot of the tower. A body of mass $2 \mathrm{M}$ thrown at a velocity $\frac{v}{2}$ from the top of the tower of height $4 \mathrm{H}$ will touch the ground at a distance of _____$\mathrm{m}$.
question: A body of mass $M$ thrown horizontally with velocity $v$ from the top of the tower of height $H$ touches the ground at a distance of $100 \mathrm{~m}$ from the foot of the tower. A body of mass $2 \mathrm{M}$ thrown at a velocity $\frac{v}{2}$ from the top of the tower of height $4 \mathrm{H}$ will touch the ground at a distance of _____$\mathrm{m}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 100=v \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{g}}} ; \quad \mathrm{x}=\frac{v}{2} \sqrt{\frac{2(4 \mathrm{H})}{\mathrm{g}}}=v \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{g}}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=100\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The angle of projection of a particle is measured from the vertical axis as $\phi$ and the maximum height reached by the particle is $h_m$. Here $h_m$ as function of $\phi$ can be presented as
question: The angle of projection of a particle is measured from the vertical axis as $\phi$ and the maximum height reached by the particle is $h_m$. Here $h_m$ as function of $\phi$ can be presented as
solution: $\mathrm{H}_{\max }=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \cos ^2 \phi}{2 \mathrm{~g}}$

subject: Physics
q: Two projectiles are thrown with same initial velocity making an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal respectively. The ratio of their respective ranges will be
question: Two projectiles are thrown with same initial velocity making an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal respectively. The ratio of their respective ranges will be
solution:
Let projection speed is u, The formula for the range of the projectile is given by, R=u2sin2θg. Therefore,

R1=u2sin90°g; R2=u2sin60°g

R1R2=sin90°sin60°=23

subject: Physics
q:
The projectile motion of a particle of mass 5 g is shown in the figure.

The initial velocity of the particle is 52 ms-1 and the air resistance is assumed to be negligible. The magnitude of the change in momentum between the points A and B is x×10-2 kgms-1. The value of x, to the nearest integer, is ___ .
question:
The projectile motion of a particle of mass 5 g is shown in the figure.

The initial velocity of the particle is 52 ms-1 and the air resistance is assumed to be negligible. The magnitude of the change in momentum between the points A and B is x×10-2 kgms-1. The value of x, to the nearest integer, is ___ .
solution:
|u→|=|v→|

u→=ucos45i^+usin45j^

v→=vcos45i^-vsin45j^

|ΔP→|=mv→-u→

ΔP=2musin45°=2×5×10-3×52×12

ΔP=50×10-3=5×10-2.

subject: Physics
q: A spring mass system (mass <math><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> spring constant <math><mi>k</mi></math> and natural length <math><mi>l</mi></math> ) rests in equilibrium on a horizontal disc. The free end of the spring is fixed at the centre of the disc. If the disc together with spring mass system rotates about it's axis with an angular velocity <math><mi>ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>></mo><mo>></mo><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> the relative change in the length of the spring is best given by the option:
question: A spring mass system (mass <math><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> spring constant <math><mi>k</mi></math> and natural length <math><mi>l</mi></math> ) rests in equilibrium on a horizontal disc. The free end of the spring is fixed at the centre of the disc. If the disc together with spring mass system rotates about it's axis with an angular velocity <math><mi>ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>></mo><mo>></mo><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> the relative change in the length of the spring is best given by the option:
solution:
mω2l0+x=kx
l0x+1=kmω2
x=l0mω2k-mω2
k≫mω2
So, xl0 is equal to mω2k
